serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of steamed rice served in a Singaporean hawker or home setting is approximately 200g of cooked rice.

nutrient_source.title: Steamed Rice — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Steamed Rice (per 100g cooked). The nutrient profile — particularly protein at 6.5g/100g, dietary fibre at 3.7g/100g, and fat at 4.21g/100g — is atypically high for plain white rice and is consistent with a mixed-grain or brown rice blend as recorded in the HPB database. All values preserved as reported by HPB.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 64 applied based on University of Sydney GI Database entry for steamed white rice (GI ~64, medium category). Given the HPB nutrient profile suggests a mixed-grain or brown rice blend (higher fibre 3.7g/100g), the actual GI may be lower (brown rice GI ~55–68); 64 is used as a conservative mid-range estimate. GL per serving calculated as: GI × carbohydrate_g per serving / 100 = 64 × 25.19 × 2 / 100 = 32.2 for a 200g bowl (50.38g carbohydrate).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 231mg/100g is borderline-high for a grain staple; however, potassium at 327mg/100g and dietary fibre at 3.7g/100g partially offset cardiovascular sodium risk.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to one bowl (200g) and avoid adding soy sauce, salt, or high-sodium condiments to the rice, as the baseline sodium of 231mg/100g is already borderline.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium GI of 64 and moderate carbohydrate load (25.19g/100g); dietary fibre at 3.7g/100g moderates postprandial glucose response compared to plain white rice.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit steamed rice to 150–180g per meal, pair with non-starchy vegetables and lean protein to blunt the glycaemic response, and prefer this mixed-grain or brown rice variant over plain white rice for its higher fibre content.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (0.8g/100g), zero trans fat, zero cholesterol, and meaningful potassium (327mg/100g) and iron (2.55mg/100g) content support a heart-healthy profile.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Steamed rice in this mixed-grain or brown rice form is acceptable for individuals with cardiovascular disease when consumed in controlled portions; the low saturated fat and zero cholesterol profile are favourable, but sodium at 231mg/100g warrants attention in salt-restricted cardiac diets.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Dietary fibre at 3.7g/100g from a whole-grain source supports gut microbiome health, which modulates immune response relevant to RA; low saturated fat does not promote inflammatory pathways.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Steamed rice of this mixed-grain or brown rice type is suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; pair with anti-inflammatory accompaniments such as turmeric-based dishes, leafy greens, and fatty fish to maximise benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Steamed Rice (蒸饭 / Nasi Kukus) is a foundational staple in Singaporean, Malay, and broader Southeast Asian cuisine. It is prepared by cooking rice grains with water using steam heat, resulting in soft, fluffy, slightly sticky grains with a mild, neutral flavour and tender texture. In Singapore, steamed rice is served at virtually every hawker centre, coffee shop, and home meal, accompanying dishes such as chicken curry, stir-fried vegetables, braised meats, and fish preparations. The HPB Singapore nutrient profile for this entry — with protein at 6.5g/100g, dietary fibre at 3.7g/100g, and fat at 4.21g/100g — is atypically elevated compared to plain white rice, suggesting this record represents a mixed-grain or brown rice blend rather than standard polished white rice. Nutritionally, per 100g cooked, it provides 164 kcal of energy, 25.19g carbohydrate, 6.5g protein, 4.21g fat, 3.7g dietary fibre, 231mg sodium, 327mg potassium, 54mg calcium, 40mg magnesium, and 2.55mg iron. The medium glycaemic index (~64) and meaningful fibre content make this a more favourable grain choice for blood glucose management compared to plain white rice. Clinically, the sodium content of 231mg/100g is borderline for hypertension management, and portion control is recommended for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The zero cholesterol and low saturated fat profile support cardiovascular health. Steamed rice is gluten-free, vegan, halal, and kosher, making it broadly suitable across dietary requirements.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a bowl of steamed rice?
Is steamed rice good for diabetics?
Steamed rice vs brown rice nutrition
Can I eat steamed rice with hypertension?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (canonical primary source for Singapore dishes, confidence 0.95 per input record). All core macros and key secondary nutrients are present. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry for steamed rice. Multilingual names verified for Singaporean cuisine requirements.
notes: 1. The nutrient profile for this HPB Steamed Rice entry — protein 6.5g/100g, dietary fibre 3.7g/100g, fat 4.21g/100g, sodium 231mg/100g — is atypically elevated compared to plain polished white rice (which typically has <2g protein, <1g fibre, <0.5g fat per 100g cooked). This profile is consistent with a mixed-grain or brown rice blend. The HPB values are preserved as reported; downstream users should verify the grain type. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this record. 3. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in the HPB source for this entry. 4. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: micronutrient detail beyond the HPB reported fields not available in the source for this specific entry. 5. saturated_fat_g of 0.8g/100g estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked brown rice (closest approved analogous source) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown; this is documented as an adapted value. 6. trans_fat_g set to 0.0: confirmed negligible/zero for plain steamed rice per USDA FoodData Central. 7. cholesterol_mg set to 0.0: confirmed zero for plant-based grain per HPB and USDA sources. 8. GI of 64 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database for steamed white rice; actual GI may be lower if grain is brown/mixed (GI ~55–68 range). GL calculated for 200g serving bowl.
